Besides
the
surfactant
effect
on
particle
size
and

drug loading,
polymer
type
and its molecular weight
also

induced different particle characterization (table 3).
PLGA
nanoparticles with Mw = 50 000 had the smallest particle size
of 247 nm compared with PLGA nanoparticles with Mw =

100 000 with a
size of 282 nm.
This
is due
to the
fact

that
increasing polymer molecular weight
leads to increasing

viscosity of
the polymer solution, which is more difﬁcult
to

break up into smaller droplets at
the same input power of

homogenization.
There was a clear
trend of drug loading
and encapsulation efﬁciency reduction when the GA/LA ratio

was
decreased.
For
example,
the
nanoparticles
prepared

with PLGA exhibited a higher
encapsulation efﬁciency of
81.3% compared with PDLLA, which had the encapsulation
efﬁciency of only 38.8%. This may be due to the fact that the

decrease of the GA unit
in the polymer backbone decreased
the hydrophilicity of the polymer, which reduced interaction

between the DNR and polymer matrix [33]. Moreover,
the
low molecular weight of PLGA showed high drug loading and
encapsulation efﬁciency.

Figures
1(a)–(c)
show
the
surface morphology
of
nanoparticles prepared with different surfactants. There were

no signiﬁcant differences
in morphology among the
three

formulations. Most nanoparticles were of
regular spherical
shape with smooth surfaces and without any aggregation or

adhesion.
It was noticeable that
the nanoparticles fabricated
with PVA and gelatin as surfactant were more homogeneous
in size and distribution, which was consistent with DLS data.

Some of
the nanoparticles prepared with Pluronic F68 had
a hollow structure, which can be observed from ﬁgure 1(a).
This may be due to the fact that Pluronic F68 produced a large
amount of foam after the homogenization of double emulsion;

then the vaporization of
the solvent and air bubbles
inside

the nanoparticles causes
the disruption of
the polymer ﬁlm
[34, 35]. These results further indicated that gelatin was better
than Pluronic F68 in particle formation.
